audit_trail.notes: All three reviewers participated in both cycles. Gemini maintained a fatal-flaw assessment across both cycles (2/10), citing mathematical trivialization of CHSH via the outcome-construction normalization and a structural measurement-independence violation as two independent fatal errors. Claude moved from 'promising' (6.0, cycle 1) to 'contested' (5.8, cycle 2) after incorporating Codex's independently derived objections about normalization bias and the logical inconsistency between the 'cannot be accounted for by local realism' claim and the acknowledged open loopholes. Codex rated the paper 'contested' in both cycles (5.6 → 5.8), agreeing with Claude on all major concerns. In cycle 2, Claude and Codex explicitly evaluated each other's objections, accepting several and dismissing two (the truncated-state superluminal-signaling concern, which the authors address in Eq. 19, and the claim that the polarizer analogy is categorically inapplicable). The Cieślński et al. PRL 2026 paper appears in the paper's own citation list and was flagged as a critical external check by all three reviewers in cycle 2; its content was not available in the review bundle.

general_reader_takeaway: This paper claims to violate a foundational quantum inequality using photons that are not conventionally entangled with each other — which, if true, would challenge prevailing explanations of why quantum mechanics conflicts with classical intuitions. The review board found the underlying optical experiment technically impressive, but the central Bell-violation claim rests on a probability-construction method that all three reviewers flagged as inadequately justified: "−1 outcomes" are inferred from separate, phase-shifted measurement runs rather than directly detected. The most serious concern is that this procedure may automatically produce an apparent violation for any high-visibility interference fringe, quantum or classical, making the result potentially a mathematical artifact rather than a genuine test of local realism. Until the methodology is independently validated — a process the community appears to have begun in a cited 2026 follow-up paper — the result should be treated as a technically interesting but foundationally contested claim.
human_spotchecks: The Cieślński et al. PRL 2026 paper ('Unquestionable Bell Theorem for Interwoven Frustrated Downconversion Processes') is cited in the paper's reference list and appears to address the same experimental platform. A specialist should determine whether this paper endorses the present protocol as valid, corrects a specific flaw in the outcome-construction step, or provides an alternative measurement strategy that avoids the inferred-outcome issue.

Supplementary section S2 contains the quantitative argument that entanglement in the full quantum state is insufficient to explain S = 2.275. A specialist should verify: (a) which entanglement measure is used, (b) which bipartition is analyzed, (c) whether higher-order SPDC terms and vacuum components are included, and (d) whether the argument is model-independent.

Does the outcome-construction method (inferring '−1' from phase-shifted runs) satisfy the CHSH classical bound of 2 under general local hidden-variable models? A specialist in Bell-inequality foundations should assess whether a formal theorem exists for this protocol type, and whether any published LHV counterexample has been demonstrated.

Does the postselected four-photon state (Eq. 1 / Eq. 15) carry nonzero bipartite entanglement across the Alice-modes {a1, a2} versus Bob-modes {b1, b2} bipartition? A partial-transpose or Schmidt-rank calculation using the full squeezed-state formalism (Eqs. 16–19) would determine whether 'unentangled photons' accurately describes the relevant operational partition.
public_summary:
Wang et al. (Science Advances, August 2025) report an experiment in which four-photon frustrated interference — realized with four spontaneous parametric down-conversion (SPDC) sources arranged for path indistinguishability — yields a Clauser–Horne–Shimony–Holt (CHSH) Bell parameter of S = 2.275 ± 0.057, exceeding the classical bound of 2 by more than four standard deviations. The paper argues that the violation cannot be attributed to quantum entanglement because momentum and frequency entanglement are actively destroyed by single-mode fiber coupling and bandpass filters before the Bell test. It instead attributes the violation to quantum indistinguishability via path identity: the four-photon event can originate from either of two indistinguishable source pairs (I+II or III+IV), and this superposition of creation processes drives the observed nonlocal phase correlations. The experiment confirms that local two-photon coincidences at each party show no phase dependence, an analogue of the marginal-independence property of standard entangled Bell experiments.

The review board identified several concerns that survived adversarial cross-review. The most widely supported objection, raised at major or fatal severity by all three reviewers, is the outcome-construction procedure (Materials and Methods, Eqs. 9–14): because the experiment has no physical device that simultaneously outputs both +1 and −1, the paper assigns "−1" outcomes by relabeling "+1" coincidences measured at phase-shifted settings (α+π, β+π). One reviewer argues this algebraically trivializes the CHSH test — the normalization procedure converts any sinusoidal fringe with visibility above 1/√2 into an apparent Bell violation regardless of classical or quantum origin — making the violation a mathematical artifact of the data-processing protocol. Two reviewers rate the same issue as a major methodological gap requiring explicit justification under local hidden-variable (LHV) models, analogous to the no-enhancement assumption in single-channel polarizer Bell tests, but note that the paper does not state or defend this assumption for its specific protocol.

A second concern, rated fatal by one reviewer, is that in frustrated interference the measurement settings (the mirror phases α and β) physically modulate the source's emission rate, not merely the readout of a pre-existing source ensemble. Bell's theorem requires that measurement choices be independent of the source state; if settings alter the source, LHV models gain an additional degree of freedom to reproduce the correlations. The other two reviewers acknowledge this as a substantive concern but frame it as an open loophole that might be addressed in future implementations rather than a structural impossibility.

Both the locality loophole (settings not spacelike separated) and the fair-sampling/postselection loophole (four-fold coincidence postselection on a low-efficiency SPDC source) are explicitly acknowledged as open by the authors, though the paper also states that "the interference cannot be accounted for by local realism" — a claim that is logically stronger than the open-loophole situation supports. The quantitative argument that residual vacuum-to-four-photon entanglement is insufficient to explain S = 2.275 is relegated to supplementary section S2, which was not available for review. The paper's own citation list includes a 2026 Physical Review Letters paper by Cieślński et al. titled "Unquestionable Bell Theorem for Interwoven Frustrated Downconversion Processes," whose title implies the current paper's version is considered improvable by the community.

On the positive side, the experimental demonstration of four-photon frustrated interference with visibilities of 0.754–0.805 (all above the 1/√2 threshold) is technically solid, the local-marginal check is well supported both theoretically and experimentally, and the work has generated substantive community engagement including multiple citing papers in 2025–2026.
reviewer_disagreements: Severity of the outcome-construction flaw: Gemini rates it fatal, arguing the normalization procedure mathematically trivializes the CHSH inequality for any high-visibility sinusoidal fringe. Claude and Codex rate it major, viewing it as a methodological gap requiring explicit justification (analogous to the no-enhancement assumption in Freedman–Clauser polarizer tests) but not necessarily a structural impossibility.

Severity of the measurement-independence concern: Gemini argues the frustrated-interference mechanism structurally violates Bell's measurement-independence assumption because settings inherently modulate source emission probability in all FI experiments, making a valid Bell test impossible in this framework. Claude and Codex acknowledge the concern but treat it as an open experimental loophole potentially addressable in future implementations rather than an inherent impossibility.

Overall verdict: Gemini rates the paper 2/10 ('major_flaw') asserting the Bell-violation claim is entirely spurious due to two fatal theoretical errors. Claude and Codex both rate it 5.8/10 ('contested'), judging the optical experiment as technically credible and the conceptual contribution as potentially significant if the methodological concerns are addressed.

strongest_objections.objection: The probability construction (Eqs. 9–14) infers '−1' outcomes from coincidence counts measured at phase-shifted settings (α+π, β+π) in separate runs, rather than detecting both outcomes from a single measurement at each fixed setting. One reviewer argues this normalization algebraically forces S > 2 for any sinusoidal fringe with visibility V > 1/√2, making the apparent Bell violation a mathematical consequence of the data-processing protocol, applicable to any sufficiently high-visibility interference whether quantum or classical. Two reviewers rate this as a major unresolved methodological gap: the no-enhancement-type assumption needed to justify this construction under general LHV models is not stated or defended in the main text.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Materials and Methods, 'Definition of probabilities', Eqs. 9–14; Results, Eqs. 3–7

strongest_objections.objection: In frustrated interference, the measurement phase settings (mirrors M1 and M2) physically modulate the source's photon-pair emission rate before photons propagate to the detectors, not merely the readout of a fixed source ensemble. One reviewer argues this constitutes a structural violation of Bell's measurement-independence assumption: if settings alter source emission, LHV models have an additional degree of freedom to reproduce S > 2 without nonlocality. Two reviewers acknowledge this as a substantive concern but treat it as an open loophole rather than a structural impossibility.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Introduction (description of frustrated interference mechanism); Results, Fig. 1B; Discussion paragraph beginning 'Conceptually, our experiment differs'

strongest_objections.objection: The fair-sampling (no-enhancement) loophole is explicitly open and unresolved. Four-fold coincidence postselection on a low-efficiency SPDC source means the detected sample need not be representative of all emitted events; a local model in which detection probability co-varies with the hidden variable could reproduce S > 2 on the detected subset.

The central novelty claim — that the violation 'cannot be described by quantum entanglement in the system' — relies entirely on supplementary section S2, which was not available for review. The entanglement measure used, the bipartition analyzed, and the threshold for 'insufficient' entanglement are all unverifiable from the main text.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Abstract; Results paragraph after Eq. 8; Section S2 (supplementary, not provided in bundle)

strongest_objections.objection: The paper states that 'the interference phenomenon we have witnessed cannot be accounted for by local realism' while simultaneously acknowledging that both the locality loophole and the fair-sampling loophole are open. These claims are logically inconsistent: with both loopholes open, a local realistic model is not excluded by the experiment.

The postselected four-mode quantum state in Eq. 1 is a superposition of Fock states across modes a1, a2, b1, b2. Whether this state carries bipartite entanglement across the Alice-modes {a1, a2} vs Bob-modes {b1, b2} bipartition is not computed in the main text. If it does, the title 'Violation of Bell inequality with unentangled photons' is misleading for the operationally relevant partition.

strongest_supported_claims: The experiment demonstrates four-photon frustrated interference with measured visibilities of 0.754–0.805, all consistently above the 1/√2 ≈ 0.707 threshold, and the internally constructed Bell parameter S = 2.275 ± 0.057 is consistent with the reported coincidence data.

Local twofold coincidences at each party (Alice's modes a1, a2 and Bob's modes b1, b2) show no phase dependence, confirmed analytically by the full squeezed-state calculation in Eq. 19 and experimentally in Fig. 3B — consistent with the no-signaling requirement.

The paper correctly identifies and explicitly acknowledges that both the locality loophole (settings in the same laboratory) and the fair-sampling/sampling loophole (four-fold coincidence postselection on a low-efficiency SPDC source) remain open in this implementation.
technical_takeaway: The paper reports S = 2.275 ± 0.057 (>4 SD) from four-photon frustrated-interference (FI) coincidences using SPDC path-identity, with local marginals correctly showing no phase dependence (confirmed analytically by Eq. 19). The critical methodological issue is that the CHSH probability distribution is assembled by inferring "−1" outcomes from separate runs at phase-shifted settings α+π and β+π (Eqs. 9–14), rather than detecting orthogonal outcomes at fixed settings. One reviewer argues this normalization procedure algebraically forces S > 2 for any fringe with visibility V > 1/√2, independent of quantum effects, and that frustrated interference structurally violates Bell's measurement-independence assumption because settings physically modulate source emission probability. Two reviewers rate these concerns as major but potentially addressable, noting that analogous single-channel polarizer Bell tests (Freedman–Clauser) are accepted with explicit no-enhancement assumptions — but argue this paper does not articulate or defend the equivalent assumption for its protocol. Both locality and fair-sampling loopholes are explicitly open; the entanglement-insufficiency argument central to the "unentangled photons" claim resides in an inaccessible supplementary section (S2); and the postselected four-mode state's bipartite Alice–Bob entanglement content is not computed in the main text.
